evals.name: bare-invocation
evals.prompt:
Read skills/work/create-work-item/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-work-item skill.

A user starts a session by invoking: /create-work-item (with no arguments)

Follow the skill's Step 0 instructions. Save your response to outputs/response.md.

Stop after your initial response. Do not simulate further user input. Do not write any work item files.
evals.expected_output: Skill invites the user to describe their problem or goal and explains what to expect from the process. Does not proceed further or write anything.

evals.name: vague-topic
evals.prompt:
Read skills/work/create-work-item/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-work-item skill.

A user invokes: /create-work-item improve things

Follow Step 0 of the skill. Save your response to outputs/response.md.

Stop after asking your clarifying question(s). Do not ask business context questions yet — wait for the vagueness to be resolved first. Do not spawn any agents.
evals.expected_output: Skill recognises 'improve things' as too vague and asks at least one clarifying question before proceeding. Does not skip ahead to business context or investigation.

evals.name: business-context-first
evals.prompt:
Read skills/work/create-work-item/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-work-item skill.

A user invokes: /create-work-item add full-text search to the docs index page

Step 0: Topic is clear — proceed.
Step 1: Ask business context questions.

Save to outputs/:
- questions.md: The exact questions you ask the user
- behavior.md: One line each — (a) did you spawn any agents before asking these questions (yes/no)? (b) how many questions did you ask?

Stop after asking questions. Do NOT spawn agents yet. Do NOT propose a work item type.
evals.expected_output: Skill asks 3-5 business context questions (problem/who affected, success criteria, constraints, etc.) before doing any investigation. No agents spawned at this step.

evals.name: model-proposes-type
evals.prompt:
Read skills/work/create-work-item/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-work-item skill.

Simulate this conversation:

User: /create-work-item add full-text search to the docs index page

[Step 0: clear topic.]
[Step 1: You ask business context questions.]

User responds to business context: "Our documentation site has no search — users must browse manually. This affects all users trying to find content. Success = users can type a query and get relevant ranked results within 2 seconds. No hard deadline. Main uncertainty is whether to use a hosted search service or build our own."

[Step 2: Investigate. For this eval, you do not need to actually spawn agents — SIMULATE their results: assume no existing similar work items found, and assume web research suggests Algolia, Fuse.js, and Pagefind are common documentation search options.]

[Step 3: Propose.]

Save to outputs/:
- proposal.md: The full structured proposal you present
- behavior.md: (a) Did you recommend a specific type with a rationale, or just list types and ask? (b) What type did you suggest?
evals.expected_output: Skill recommends a specific work item type (likely 'story') with a one-sentence rationale, rather than presenting a bare list and asking the user to choose blind. Proposal includes requirements and acceptance criteria.

evals.name: challenge-untestable-ac
evals.prompt:
Read skills/work/create-work-item/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-work-item skill.

Simulate this conversation:

User: /create-work-item add full-text search to the docs index page

[Steps 0-2: topic clear, business context gathered, investigation done (simulate: no existing work items, standard search options found via research).]

[Step 3: You present a proposal with requirements and acceptance criteria.]

User response to proposal: "Looks right. Add one more acceptance criterion: 'Search should work correctly'"

Now respond to the user's proposed addition. Save to outputs/:
- response.md: Your exact response to the addition
- behavior.md: (a) Did you accept 'should work correctly' as-is? (b) Did you push back and ask for something measurable? (c) What did you ask for instead?
evals.expected_output: Skill does not accept 'should work correctly' as a valid acceptance criterion. It pushes back, explains why it's untestable, and asks what a passing test would look like.

evals.name: bug-type-requirements
evals.prompt:
Read skills/work/create-work-item/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-work-item skill.

Simulate through to draft generation:

User: /create-work-item login page throws 500 error when password contains special characters

[Step 0: clear topic.]
[Step 1: Ask business context questions.]

User: "This affects users who set passwords with !, @, # etc. They get a 500 error after submitting the login form. Started happening after last week's password validation update. Reproduced on production with Chrome on macOS. Very high impact — users with special chars in passwords cannot log in at all. No workaround."

[Step 2: Investigate. Simulate: no existing similar work items found.]
[Step 3: Propose. You should recognise this as a bug.]

User: "Proposal looks right, approve."

[Step 4: Generate the draft work item.]

Save to outputs/:
- draft.md: Full work item draft content
- behavior.md: (a) What type was proposed? (b) Does Requirements contain reproduction steps? (c) Is the Requirements section renamed?

Do NOT call work-item-next-number.sh. Use XXXX as the work item number.
evals.expected_output: Type 'bug' proposed. Requirements section contains reproduction steps, expected behaviour, actual behaviour, and environment. Section is not renamed.

evals.name: draft-xxxx-placeholder
evals.prompt:
Read skills/work/create-work-item/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-work-item skill.

Simulate through to draft generation:

User: /create-work-item add CSV export to the reporting dashboard

[Step 0: clear.]
[Step 1: Ask business context questions.]

User: "Finance team needs to pull report data into Excel. Currently they screenshot or manually copy. Success = one-click CSV download from any report view. High priority, no hard deadline."

[Step 2: Investigate. Simulate: no existing work items found.]
[Step 3: Propose.]

User: "Good proposal, approved."

[Step 4: Generate the draft work item.]

Save to outputs/:
- draft.md: Full work item draft content
- behavior.md: (a) Does the draft use XXXX as the work item ID? (b) Does the draft contain any real work item number (0001, 0002, etc.)?

CRITICAL: Do NOT call work-item-next-number.sh at any point. Use XXXX as the placeholder.
evals.expected_output: Draft work item uses XXXX throughout as the work item number placeholder. work-item-next-number.sh is never called. All eight frontmatter fields are populated, with work_item_id: XXXX.

evals.name: number-not-consumed
evals.prompt:
Read skills/work/create-work-item/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-work-item skill.

Simulate through to draft presentation WITHOUT the user approving:

User: /create-work-item add password strength meter to signup form

[Step 0: clear.]
[Step 1: Ask business context questions.]

User: "Security team wants stronger passwords. Affects all new signups. Success = visible strength meter during signup that blocks weak passwords from being submitted."

[Step 2: Investigate. Simulate: no existing work items.]
[Step 3: Propose.]

User: "Proposal looks good."

[Step 4: Present the draft work item.]

The session ends here — the user does not say 'approve', 'yes', or provide explicit approval. The session simply ends.

Save to outputs/behavior.md:
- Was work-item-next-number.sh called at any point? (yes/no)
- If yes, at what step?
- Was any work item file written to disk? (yes/no)

Do NOT call work-item-next-number.sh. Do NOT write any file to disk.
evals.expected_output: work-item-next-number.sh is never called. No work item file is written. The skill shows the draft and waits — it requires explicit approval before proceeding to Step 5.

evals.name: near-duplicate-surfaced
evals.prompt:
Read skills/work/create-work-item/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-work-item skill.

Simulate this conversation through Step 2:

User: /create-work-item add search to our documentation site

[Step 0: clear.]
[Step 1: Ask business context questions.]

User: "Users struggle to find specific docs. Search would help. No deadline."

[Step 2: Investigate. SIMULATE the documents locator agent returning this result — do NOT actually spawn an agent:
Found in meta/work/: 0001-add-search-to-docs.md
Title: Add full-text search to documentation index
Status: draft]

Now handle the near-duplicate finding per the skill's instructions.

Save to outputs/:
- response.md: Your exact response when the similar work item is found
- behavior.md: (a) Did you surface the existing work item? (b) What options did you offer the user? (c) Did you silently continue creating a new work item?
evals.expected_output: Skill surfaces the existing work item and asks whether to proceed with a new work item or exit to update the existing one manually. Does not silently continue.

evals.name: epic-type-requirements
evals.prompt:
Read skills/work/create-work-item/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-work-item skill.

Simulate through to draft generation:

User: /create-work-item user authentication overhaul

[Step 0: This has a clear subject (auth overhaul). Proceed.]
[Step 1: Ask business context questions.]

User: "We need to replace basic auth with a modern system. Affects all users. Success = SSO support, MFA options, and improved session management all shipped. This is a major initiative spanning multiple teams and will decompose into many sub-stories."

[Step 2: Investigate. Simulate: no existing work items.]
[Step 3: Propose. This should be recognised as an epic.]

User: "Good proposal, approved."

[Step 4: Generate the draft work item.]

Save to outputs/:
- draft.md: Full work item draft content
- behavior.md: (a) What type was proposed? (b) Does the Requirements section contain initial story decomposition? (c) Is the Requirements section renamed (e.g. to 'Stories')? (d) Is the Acceptance Criteria section absent or minimal?
evals.expected_output: Type 'epic' proposed. Requirements section contains initial story breakdown. Section is NOT renamed. Acceptance Criteria is absent or minimal for epics.

evals.name: drafting-notes-populated
evals.prompt:
Read skills/work/create-work-item/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-work-item skill.

Simulate through to draft generation:

User: /create-work-item add a way for users to export their data

[Step 0: clear topic.]
[Step 1: Ask business context questions.]

User: "Users have been asking for this. It's about giving them control over their data. Success = users can get their data out whenever they want. No hard deadline."

Note the vagueness: 'users', 'their data', 'get it out' — each of these admits multiple interpretations.

[Step 2: Investigate. Simulate: no existing work items; web research confirms common approaches include JSON export, CSV export, and GDPR-style data portability.]

[Step 3: Propose. The user responded terse. You will have to interpret: which users (end users vs. admins?), what data (profile? activity? everything?), what format (JSON/CSV/archive?), and whether this is a feature or a compliance obligation.]

User: "Looks reasonable, approved."

[Step 4: Generate the draft work item.]

Save to outputs/:
- proposal.md: The structured proposal you presented in Step 3
- draft.md: Full work item draft content
- behavior.md: (a) Did the proposal include a Drafting Notes section? (b) Did the written draft include a Drafting Notes section? (c) List each Drafting Note entry in the final draft verbatim. (d) Do any of them capture interpretations about scope (which users, which data), stakeholders, terminology, or technical approach?

Do NOT call work-item-next-number.sh. Use XXXX as the placeholder.
evals.expected_output: Both the proposal and the final draft include a Drafting Notes section with substantive entries covering the user's ambiguous terms (which users, what data, what format, feature vs compliance). Drafting Notes are distinct from Assumptions and Open Questions.

evals.name: author-resolution
evals.prompt:
Read skills/work/create-work-item/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-work-item skill.

The current git/jj user is: Toby Clemson <toby@go-atomic.io>

Simulate a full run through to writing:

User: /create-work-item add dark mode toggle to settings

[Step 0: clear.]
[Step 1: ask business context questions.]

User: "Users have requested this repeatedly. Affects anyone using the app in low light. Success = users can toggle between light/dark themes and the choice persists across sessions."

[Step 2: Investigate. Simulate: no existing work items.]
[Step 3: Propose.]

User: "Good proposal, approved."

[Step 4: Generate the draft work item.]

User: "Looks good, approved — write it."

[Step 5: Write the work item. For this eval, simulate work-item-next-number.sh returning 0042. Do not actually call the script. The work items directory is meta/work/.]

Save to outputs/:
- final-work item.md: The final content written to disk (with 0042 substituted for XXXX)
- behavior.md: (a) What value did you put in the 'author' frontmatter field? (b) Did you ask the user for the author name during the session, or did you resolve it automatically? (c) Does the 'author' field contain the literal string [author] or similar placeholder?

You may simulate reading the git user identity as 'Toby Clemson' without actually running a command.
evals.expected_output: The written work item has author 'Toby Clemson' (resolved from git identity) rather than a [author] placeholder or an asked value. The skill does not ask the user for author when the git identity is available.

evals.name: template-driven-types
evals.prompt:
Read skills/work/create-work-item/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-work-item skill.

IMPORTANT: For this eval, pretend the work item template's 'type' field was updated earlier today to include a new type called 'chore' for routine operational work (in addition to the usual story/epic/task/bug/spike). The template frontmatter now reads: 'type: story  # story | epic | task | bug | spike | chore'. You should NOT need to assume only the five original types exist — read the template output and use whatever types it exposes.

Simulate this:

User: /create-work-item rotate the production API keys quarterly

[Step 0: clear topic — a recurring operational task.]
[Step 1: Ask business context questions.]

User: "Security team mandates quarterly key rotation. Affects our production systems. Success = rotation happens on schedule without service interruption. This is routine ops work, not a feature."

[Step 2: Investigate. Simulate: no existing work items.]
[Step 3: Propose. Consider all types available in the template, including 'chore' if it's a better fit than 'task'.]

Save to outputs/:
- proposal.md: Your structured proposal from Step 3
- behavior.md: (a) What type did you propose? (b) Did you consider 'chore' as a valid type? (c) Did you treat the type list as open-ended (read from template) or did you stick to a hardcoded five-type list?

Stop after the proposal. Do NOT call work-item-next-number.sh.
evals.expected_output: Skill proposes 'chore' (or at least considers it) because the template — not a hardcoded list — defines the valid types. Skill does not dismiss 'chore' as invalid on the grounds it wasn't in a memorised list.

evals.name: references-populated
evals.prompt:
Read skills/work/create-work-item/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-work-item skill.

Simulate through to draft generation:

User: /create-work-item evaluate options for replacing our search backend

[Step 0: clear topic.]
[Step 1: Ask business context questions.]

User: "Our current search is Elasticsearch 6 which is end-of-life. We need to pick a replacement. Affects all users who search the site. Success = we've chosen a path forward with a documented rationale. No deadline but sooner is better."

[Step 2: Investigate. SIMULATE these results — do NOT actually spawn agents:
- documents-locator returns: no similar work items in meta/work/.
- web-search-researcher returns: https://opensearch.org/ (Elasticsearch fork), https://typesense.org/, https://www.meilisearch.com/, and a blog post at https://example.com/2025/elasticsearch-eol discussing migration paths.]

[Step 3: Propose as a spike given the uncertainty.]

User: "Good proposal, approved."

[Step 4: Generate the draft work item.]

Save to outputs/:
- draft.md: Full work item draft content
- behavior.md: (a) Does the draft contain a References section? (b) List each reference in the References section verbatim. (c) Are the research URLs/sources from Step 2 reflected in References?

Do NOT call work-item-next-number.sh. Use XXXX as the placeholder.
evals.expected_output: The draft's References section lists the research URLs surfaced in Step 2 (OpenSearch, Typesense, Meilisearch, the blog post). The References section is present and populated, not empty or omitted. No invented references.

evals.name: enrich-existing-path-like
evals.prompt:
Read skills/work/create-work-item/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-work-item skill.

A user invokes: /create-work-item skills/work/create-work-item/evals/files/0042-existing-sparse.md

The file at that path exists and is available to you — read it.

Follow Step 0 of the skill. The argument contains '/' so it is path-like. Resolve it, validate the frontmatter, enter enrich-existing mode, and produce your initial response.

Save your response to outputs/response.md.

Stop after your initial response. Do not simulate further user input. Do not write any work item files.
evals.expected_output: Skill recognises the path-like argument, reads the file, validates its frontmatter, and enters enrich-existing mode. The initial response mentions the file's existing title or work_item_id (0042 / 'Add Full-Text Search to Documentation Index'). The skill does not ask topic-clarification questions.
expectations: Response cites the file's existing title or work_item_id (0042)
Response does not ask 'what do you want to achieve' or similar topic-clarification questions
Response indicates the skill is in enrich mode (e.g. mentions reading the existing file or reviewing it for gaps)

evals.name: enrich-existing-numeric-single-match
evals.prompt:
Read skills/work/create-work-item/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-work-item skill.

A user invokes: /create-work-item 42

The work items directory is skills/work/create-work-item/evals/files/ for this eval. Treat a glob of that directory for '0042-*.md' as returning a single match: skills/work/create-work-item/evals/files/0042-existing-sparse.md. Read that file.

Follow Step 0. The argument '42' is numeric. Zero-pad to 0042. The glob returns one match. Validate frontmatter and enter enrich-existing mode.

Save your response to outputs/response.md.

Stop after your initial response. Do not simulate further user input. Do not write any work item files.
evals.expected_output: Skill recognises the numeric argument 42, zero-pads to 0042, finds the single matching file, reads it, validates frontmatter, and enters enrich-existing mode. The initial response mentions the file's title or ID. No topic-clarification questions.
expectations: Response cites the resolved file's work_item_id (0042) or title
Response does not ask topic-clarification questions
Response indicates enrich mode — reading the existing file and preparing to identify gaps

evals.name: enrich-existing-numeric-multi-match
evals.prompt:
Read skills/work/create-work-item/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-work-item skill.

A user invokes: /create-work-item 42

The work items directory for this eval is skills/work/create-work-item/evals/files/. Treat a glob of that directory for '0042-*.md' as returning TWO matches:
  1. skills/work/create-work-item/evals/files/0042-existing-sparse.md
  2. skills/work/create-work-item/evals/files/0042-other-slug.md

Both files are available to you — you may read them.

Follow Step 0. Multiple matches exist. List numbered options and ask the user to select.

Save your response to outputs/response.md.

Stop after listing the options. Do not simulate the user selecting one.
evals.expected_output: Skill lists two numbered options (the two matching files) and asks the user to select by number or full path. Does not proceed to read either file or enter enrich mode until the user selects.
expectations: Response lists both matching files as numbered options
Response asks the user to select a file by number or specify the full path
Response does not proceed into enrich mode — waits for user selection

evals.name: enrich-existing-missing-path-fallback
evals.prompt:
Read skills/work/create-work-item/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-work-item skill.

A user invokes: /create-work-item meta/work/9999-nope.md

The argument is path-like (contains '/'). The file at meta/work/9999-nope.md does NOT exist.

Follow Step 0. Print the missing-path fallback warning naming the input path explicitly, then proceed into topic-string handling (ask business-context questions about the path treated as a topic, or ask for clarification).

Save your response to outputs/response.md.

Stop after your initial response.
evals.expected_output: Skill prints a warning that names the input path 'meta/work/9999-nope.md' explicitly, notes it is being interpreted as a topic string, and proceeds into topic-string Step 0 logic (either recognising the pseudo-path as a vague topic and asking a clarifying question, or treating it as a topic and asking business-context questions).
expectations: Response includes a warning that names the input path meta/work/9999-nope.md explicitly
Response indicates the input is being interpreted as a topic string
Conversation continues into topic-string Step 0 logic — does not abort

evals.name: enrich-existing-unparseable-frontmatter
evals.prompt:
Read skills/work/create-work-item/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-work-item skill.

A user invokes: /create-work-item skills/work/create-work-item/evals/files/0099-broken-frontmatter.md

The file exists and is available — read it. Note that its frontmatter is unclosed (no closing '---' line).

Follow Step 0. The path resolves to a file. Run work-item-read-field.sh (or simulate its failure) — it will exit non-zero because the frontmatter is unclosed. Print the unparseable-frontmatter diagnostic and abort. Do NOT fall back to topic-string handling.

Save your response to outputs/response.md.

Stop after the error message. Do not simulate further user input.
evals.expected_output: Skill prints the unparseable-frontmatter diagnostic (containing 'Could not parse frontmatter') and aborts. No Step 1 questions follow. No fallback to topic-string handling.
expectations: Response prints the unparseable-frontmatter diagnostic containing the substring 'Could not parse frontmatter'
Conversation aborts — no Step 1 business-context or gap-analysis questions follow
Response does NOT fall back to topic-string handling

evals.name: enrich-existing-numeric-no-match-fallback
evals.prompt:
Read skills/work/create-work-item/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-work-item skill.

A user invokes: /create-work-item 9999

The argument matches the numeric discriminator (^[0-9]+$). Zero-pad to 9999. Treat a glob of the work items directory for '9999-*.md' as returning zero matches.

Follow Step 0. Print the no-match fallback warning naming the number explicitly, then proceed into topic-string handling.

Save your response to outputs/response.md.

Stop after your initial response.
evals.expected_output: Skill prints a warning naming the number 9999 explicitly, notes it is being interpreted as a topic string, and proceeds into topic-string Step 0 logic (asks business-context or clarifying questions about the topic).
expectations: Response includes a warning that names the input number 9999 explicitly
Response indicates the input is being interpreted as a topic string
Conversation continues into topic-string Step 0 logic — does not abort

evals.name: enrich-existing-gap-analysis
evals.prompt:
Read skills/work/create-work-item/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-work-item skill.

A user invokes: /create-work-item skills/work/create-work-item/evals/files/0042-existing-sparse.md

The file exists and is available — read it fully.

Follow Step 0: path-like argument, file exists, frontmatter validates (work_item_id: 0042). Enter enrich-existing mode.

Follow Step 1 in enrich-existing mode: identify which body sections are substantive (real content beyond [bracketed placeholder] text) and which are gaps. Tag each gap with exactly one of: empty, placeholder-only, instructional-prose, or partial. Present the gap analysis in the specified format, then ask targeted questions about the identified gaps.

Save to outputs/:
- response.md: Your full Step 1 response including gap analysis and follow-up questions.

Stop after asking your gap-targeted questions. Do not simulate user answers.
evals.expected_output: Skill presents a gap analysis listing Complete and Gaps sections. Each gap is tagged with one of the four literal tokens (empty, placeholder-only, instructional-prose, partial). Follow-up questions target only the identified gaps, not broad discovery questions.
expectations: Response contains a 'Complete:' listing and a 'Gaps:' listing
Each gap entry is tagged with exactly one of: empty, placeholder-only, instructional-prose, or partial
Step 1 follow-up questions target only sections listed under Gaps
Transcript shows no broad-discovery questions (the default Step 1 vagueness check is not run)

evals.name: enrich-existing-gap-mixed-section
evals.prompt:
Read skills/work/create-work-item/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-work-item skill.

A user invokes: /create-work-item skills/work/create-work-item/evals/files/0042-existing-mixed.md

The file exists and is available — read it fully. Note that the Summary section contains one substantive sentence ('Users need a way to search for content...') followed by a residual [bracketed] placeholder on the next line. All other body sections are fully bracketed placeholders.

Follow Step 0: path-like, file exists, frontmatter validates. Enter enrich-existing mode.

Follow Step 1 in enrich-existing mode: identify gaps, tag each with the appropriate literal token. The mixed Summary section should be classified as 'partial' (one substantive sentence alongside a residual placeholder).

Save to outputs/:
- response.md: Your gap analysis and follow-up questions.

Stop after asking questions.
evals.expected_output: The mixed Summary section (one substantive sentence plus a residual placeholder) is classified as a gap with reason 'partial'. A follow-up question targets that section. Other fully-bracketed sections are classified as empty or placeholder-only.
expectations: The Summary section is classified as a gap with the tag 'partial'
Step 1 asks a follow-up question about the Summary section
Other fully-bracketed sections are tagged 'empty' or 'placeholder-only', not 'partial'

evals.name: enrich-existing-gap-instructional-prose
evals.prompt:
Read skills/work/create-work-item/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-work-item skill.

A user invokes: /create-work-item skills/work/create-work-item/evals/files/0042-existing-instructional.md

The file exists and is available — read it fully. Note that the Summary section contains the template's instructional prose verbatim: 'Describe the business value of this work item here. Explain what problem it solves and who benefits from the solution.' All other body sections are standard bracketed placeholders.

Follow Step 0: path-like, file exists, frontmatter validates. Enter enrich-existing mode.

Follow Step 1 in enrich-existing mode: identify gaps. The instructional-prose Summary must be classified as a gap with tag 'instructional-prose'.

Save to outputs/:
- response.md: Your gap analysis and follow-up questions.

Stop after asking questions.
evals.expected_output: The Summary section containing verbatim template instructional prose is classified as a gap with the tag 'instructional-prose'. Follow-up questions target it.
expectations: The Summary section is classified as a gap with the tag 'instructional-prose'
Step 1 asks a follow-up question targeting the instructional-prose Summary
The instructional-prose section is NOT classified as 'partial' or 'placeholder-only'

evals.name: enrich-existing-rich-confirms
evals.prompt:
Read skills/work/create-work-item/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-work-item skill.

A user invokes: /create-work-item skills/work/create-work-item/evals/files/0042-existing-rich.md

The file exists and is available — read it fully. It contains a fully-fleshed work item with substantive content in all sections.

Follow Step 0: path-like, file exists, frontmatter validates. Enter enrich-existing mode.

Follow Step 1 in enrich-existing mode. The existing content is rich enough that no obvious gaps remain. Briefly confirm this and ask what the user wants to add or improve.

Save to outputs/:
- response.md: Your Step 1 response.

Stop after your response. Do not simulate user answers.
evals.expected_output: Skill confirms there are no obvious gaps in the rich work item and asks what the user wants to add or improve. Does not run the default Step 1 broad-discovery questions.
expectations: Response confirms there are no obvious gaps
Response asks what the user wants to add or improve
Response does not re-ask the default Step 1 discovery questions (e.g. 'what pain point', 'what is the desired outcome')

evals.name: enrich-existing-self-not-duplicate
evals.prompt:
Read skills/work/create-work-item/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-work-item skill.

Simulate this conversation in enrich-existing mode:

User invokes: /create-work-item skills/work/create-work-item/evals/files/0042-existing-sparse.md

[Step 0: path-like, file resolves, frontmatter validates. Enter enrich-existing mode with existing_work_item_path = skills/work/create-work-item/evals/files/0042-existing-sparse.md, work_item_id = 0042, title = 'Add Full-Text Search to Documentation Index'.]

[Step 1 in enrich-existing mode: gap analysis run. User answers gap questions.]

User: "The search should work across all our documentation pages. No constraints, just get something working."

[Step 2: SIMULATE the documents-locator agent returning the input file itself as a search result:

evals.name: enrich-existing-oblique-status-mention
evals.prompt:
Read skills/work/create-work-item/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-work-item skill.

User invokes: /create-work-item skills/work/create-work-item/evals/files/0042-existing-sparse.md

[Step 2: no duplicates.]
[Step 3 enrich mode: the user's message contains an oblique remark 'this is now in flight'. This is NOT a direct request to set status. The model must ask a clarifying question about the user's status intent rather than inferring a transition.]

Save to outputs/:
- response.md: Your Step 3 response.
- behavior.md: (a) Did the model ask a clarifying question about the status remark? (b) Did the model infer and propose a status transition without asking?
evals.expected_output: Model asks a clarifying question about whether 'now in flight' means the user wants to change the status (e.g. to 'in-progress'), rather than inferring the transition. The model does not unilaterally propose 'draft → in-progress'.
expectations: Model asks a clarifying question about the user's status intent rather than inferring a transition
Model does NOT unilaterally propose a status transition (e.g. draft → in-progress) without a clarifying question
